ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general dumpling food product class; no specific fried handmade dumpling subclass exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers dumplings generically; no fried-specific or handmade-specific subcode identified. closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for fried handmade dumplings as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried handmade dumplings consists of approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 150 g, commonly served as a snack or starter.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Dumplings (Jiaozi, fried) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Asian dumpling preparations
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'fried handmade dumplings' exists in a single approved source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for fried dumplings / potstickers (NDB 18033, similar entries) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Asian wheat-dough filled snacks. Energy and macros reflect a pork-and-vegetable filled, deep-fried wheat-wrapper dumpling at approximately 55–60% wrapper and 40–45% filling by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat-based filled dumplings / dim sum analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fried handmade dumplings was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated by analogy with wheat-flour-based filled dim sum and pasta-type products (GI range 40–55 in Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The wheat wrapper contributes the primary glycemic load; frying and protein/fat content from the filling lower the effective GI relative to plain wheat products. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 44.25) / 100 ≈ 21.2, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 29.5 × 1.5 = 44.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~630 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 3–4 pieces and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce, as a single 150 g serving already contributes approximately 27% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~21 per serving) and refined wheat wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size; the refined wheat wrapper raises postprandial glucose, and a 150 g serving delivers approximately 44 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~21, which is at the upper boundary of the moderate GL range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat content from deep-frying (~12 g fat per 100 g, including ~2.8 g saturated fat) and sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, fried dumplings should be consumed occasionally and in small portions due to the combined burden of saturated fat from the filling and absorbed frying oil, alongside the moderate sodium content that may contribute to blood pressure elevation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried handmade dumplings are a popular Asian snack and dim sum item consisting of hand-formed wheat-flour dough wrappers filled with a seasoned mixture of minced pork or chicken, shredded cabbage, spring onion, ginger, sesame oil, and soy sauce. The filled parcels are deep-fried until the exterior wrapper becomes golden-brown and crispy, while the interior filling remains moist and savoury. The texture contrast between the crunchy shell and the juicy filling is a defining characteristic. Originating from Chinese culinary tradition, fried dumplings (known as 炸饺子 or 煎饺 in Chinese) are widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia and have become a staple snack in Singapore's hawker and restaurant culture. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 265 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 12 g fat, and 29.5 g carbohydrate, with a moderate sodium content of ~420 mg. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low GI), though the glycemic load per standard serving of 6 pieces (~150 g) is approximately 21, placing it in the moderate GL range. Clinically, the sodium and fat content from frying are the primary concerns for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate glycemic load warrants portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes. Fried dumplings contain gluten and soy allergens and are not suitable for vegetarians or vegans when filled with meat.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'fried handmade dumplings' was found in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried dumpling/potsticker entries and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Medium confidence reflects the adapted/estimated nature of the data rather than a direct exact match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this adapted record. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source for this food category. All other -1 fields reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted source composite. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour filled dim sum and pasta-type products from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct measurement for fried handmade dumplings was located. Halal status is marked false as the default filling contains pork; halal variants exist but cannot be assumed for this generic record. The input record listed cuisine tags including Ramen, Pizza, and Pasta which are inconsistent with this food item and have been disregarded. Cuisine has been corrected to Asian.
